questions.question:
Four cards lie on a table. Each card has a number printed on one side and a colour on the other. The faces visible on the cards are 2, 3, red, and blue.

Proposition: If a card has an even value on one side, then its opposite face is red.

The card(s) which MUST be turned over to verify the above proposition are

A. 2, red
B. 2, 3, red
C. 2, blue
D. 2, red, blue

understand.plain: We need to verify a conditional: even number => red on back. A conditional P => Q is violated only when P is true and Q is false. So we must check any card that could be 'even on one side' (to see if its back is NOT red) and any card that could be 'not red' (to see if its front is even). Card '2' is even, so we must flip it to check its colour. Card 'blue' is not red, so we must flip it to check if it has an even number. Card '3' is odd so its back does not matter. Card 'red' already satisfies the consequent regardless of its number.

keywords.explain: A statement 'if P then Q' is falsified only when P is true and Q is false. Checking the rule means looking for potential falsifiers.
keywords.example: 'If it rains, the ground is wet.' To check this, look for rain without wet ground, not for dry ground with no rain.

keywords.explain: A logic puzzle where you must identify which cards (or cases) could falsify a given conditional rule. Most people wrongly check the confirming case (red) instead of the falsifying case (blue).
keywords.example: Rule: even => red. Falsifier: even number with non-red (blue) back. So flip '2' and flip 'blue'.

keywords.explain: When the antecedent is false, the conditional is automatically true. Odd card '3' cannot make the rule false, so no need to flip it.
keywords.example: Card '3' is odd (P is false), so whatever colour is on back, the rule is not violated.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='420' height='160' viewBox='0 0 420 160' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='20' width='80' height='100' rx='8' class='hi'/><text x='50' y='78' text-anchor='middle' class='big ink'>2</text><text x='50' y='110' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ok'>FLIP</text><rect x='110' y='20' width='80' height='100' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='150' y='78' text-anchor='middle' class='big ink'>3</text><text x='150' y='110' text-anchor='middle' class='tag mut'>SKIP</text><rect x='210' y='20' width='80' height='100' rx='8' class='bg' style='fill:#FFCCCC;'/><text x='250' y='78' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>red</text><text x='250' y='110' text-anchor='middle' class='tag mut'>SKIP</text><rect x='310' y='20' width='80' height='100' rx='8' class='hi'/><text x='350' y='78' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>blue</text><text x='350' y='110' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ok'>FLIP</text><text x='210' y='155' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Flip cards that could falsify: even number or non-red colour</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Four playing cards labelled 2, 3, red, blue with cards '2' and 'blue' highlighted as the ones to flip.

given.aim: Identify which card(s) must be flipped to verify the proposition 'even number => red on back'.

terms.meaning: If a card has an even value, its opposite face is red (even => red).
terms.example: Card showing 4 must have red on back; card showing 5 can have any colour.
terms.connects: Defines the conditional we are testing.

terms.meaning: The rule is broken only when a card is even on one side AND not red on the other.
terms.example: A card showing 2 with blue on the back would falsify the rule.
terms.connects: Drives the selection: look only at cards that could produce this violation.

terms.meaning: The four visible faces are: 2 (even), 3 (odd), red (colour), blue (colour).
terms.example: Card '2' could have blue on back (violation). Card 'blue' could have an even number on back (violation).
terms.connects: Only '2' and 'blue' can be part of a violating pair.

given.plan: For each visible face, ask: can this card be a counterexample to the rule? Check card '2' (even, so back must be red), check card 'blue' (not red, so front must not be even). Cards '3' and 'red' cannot falsify the rule.

steps.title: Identify which visible faces could be counterexamples
steps.formula_raw: P => Q is false only when P is true and Q is false
steps.apply: P = 'even number', Q = 'back is red'. Counterexample: even number AND back is NOT red.
steps.note: Only cards that are even (could make P true) or non-red (could make Q false) are candidates.

steps.title: Card '2': must flip
steps.formula_raw: P => Q is false only when P is true and Q is false
steps.apply: Card shows 2 (even). If back is blue or any non-red, the rule is violated. Must flip to verify back is red.
steps.note: This is the standard confirming check: does the even card have red on back?

steps.title: Card '3': skip
steps.formula_raw: An implication with a false antecedent is automatically true
steps.apply: Card shows 3 (odd). P is false regardless of back colour. Rule cannot be violated. No need to flip.
steps.note: Vacuous truth: odd => rule holds automatically.

steps.title: Card 'red': skip
steps.formula_raw: P => Q is false only when P is true and Q is false
steps.apply: Card shows red. Q is already true (back is red). Rule cannot be violated regardless of the number on back.
steps.note: Even if the other side is 2, the condition is satisfied.

steps.title: Card 'blue': must flip
steps.formula_raw: (A -> B) is logically equivalent to (not B -> not A)
steps.apply: Card shows blue (not red). By contrapositive: if back is red is false, then front must not be even. Must flip to check that back is not an even number.
steps.note: This is the counterintuitive but critical card: blue means back could be even, which violates the rule.

steps.title: Conclude: flip 2 and blue
steps.formula_raw: even => red  is equivalent to  not-red => not-even
steps.apply: Cards to flip: 2 (check it has red back) and blue (check it does not have an even number on back). Answer: C.
steps.note: Options A and D wrongly include 'red' which cannot be a counterexample.

questions.formula_note: The conditional 'even => red' is falsified only by an even-numbered card with a non-red back; the contrapositive drives why 'blue' must also be checked.

questions.question:
A rule states that in order to drink beer, one must be over 18 years old. In a bar, there are 4 people: P is 16 years old, Q is 25 years old, R is drinking milkshake, and S is drinking a beer. What must be checked to ensure that the rule is being followed?

A. Only P's drink
B. Only P's drink and S's age
C. Only S's age
D. Only P's drink, Q's drink, and S's age
questions.answer: B. Only P's drink and S's age

understand.plain: The rule is: drink beer => age > 18. To verify this rule, we look for potential violations: a person who drinks beer but is not over 18. P is 16 (underage) so we must check whether P is drinking beer. S is already drinking beer so we must check whether S is over 18. Q is 25 (over 18) so whatever Q drinks does not violate the rule. R is drinking milkshake (not beer) so the rule does not apply to R regardless of age.

keywords.explain: Rule: drink_beer => age > 18. A violation is: drink_beer AND age <= 18. Check only those who could create a violation.
keywords.example: P could drink beer (unknown) and is underage. S is drinking beer and could be underage. These are the two checks needed.

keywords.explain: If a person is not drinking beer, the rule is trivially satisfied for them regardless of age.
keywords.example: R drinks milkshake. The rule does not apply. No need to check R's age.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='460' height='180' viewBox='0 0 460 180' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='20' width='95' height='120' rx='8' class='hi'/><text x='57' y='50' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>P</text><text x='57' y='75' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>age 16</text><text x='57' y='95' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>drink: ?</text><text x='57' y='120' text-anchor='middle' class='tag no'>Check drink</text><rect x='120' y='20' width='95' height='120' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='167' y='50' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Q</text><text x='167' y='75' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>age 25</text><text x='167' y='95' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>drink: ?</text><text x='167' y='120' text-anchor='middle' class='tag mut'>Safe (adult)</text><rect x='230' y='20' width='95' height='120' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='277' y='50' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>R</text><text x='277' y='75' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>age: ?</text><text x='277' y='95' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>milkshake</text><text x='277' y='120' text-anchor='middle' class='tag mut'>Safe (no beer)</text><rect x='340' y='20' width='95' height='120' rx='8' class='hi'/><text x='387' y='50' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>S</text><text x='387' y='75' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>age: ?</text><text x='387' y='95' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>beer</text><text x='387' y='120' text-anchor='middle' class='tag no'>Check age</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Four people P, Q, R, S with P and S highlighted as needing verification — P's drink must be checked and S's age must be checked.

given.aim: Determine whose drink or age must be verified to enforce the rule 'beer => age > 18'.

terms.meaning: Drinking beer requires age > 18. Formally: drink_beer(x) => age(x) > 18.
terms.example: If someone is drinking beer and is 16, the rule is violated.
terms.connects: Defines the conditional we must enforce.

terms.meaning: P is underage. If P is drinking beer, the rule is violated. We must check P's drink.
terms.example: If P drinks beer => violation. If P drinks soda => no issue.
terms.connects: P's drink is an unknown that could create a violation.

terms.meaning: S is already drinking beer. The rule requires S to be over 18. We must check S's age.
terms.example: If S is 16 => violation. If S is 21 => no issue.
terms.connects: S's age is the unknown that could create a violation.

terms.term: Q (age 25, drink unknown) and R (milkshake, age unknown)
terms.meaning: Q is over 18, so whatever Q drinks does not violate the rule. R is not drinking beer, so the rule is not triggered.
terms.example: Q drinking beer at 25 is fine. R at any age with milkshake is fine.
terms.connects: Neither Q nor R can create a violation given current knowledge.

given.plan: Apply the conditional 'drink_beer => age > 18'. A violation needs: beer AND age <= 18. Identify people where one condition is known to be true and the other is unknown.

steps.title: Restate rule as a conditional
steps.formula_raw: P => Q is false only when P is true and Q is false
steps.apply: Rule: drink_beer(x) => age(x) > 18. Violation: drink_beer(x) is TRUE and age(x) > 18 is FALSE.
steps.note: Only persons where a violation is possible need checking.

steps.title: Check P: age 16, drink unknown
steps.formula_raw: P => Q is false only when P is true and Q is false
steps.apply: P's age is 16 (<= 18, so Q is false). If P drinks beer (P is true), rule is violated. Must check P's drink.
steps.note: P's age already violates the consequent; only way to be safe is if P is NOT drinking beer.

steps.title: Check Q: age 25, drink unknown
steps.formula_raw: P => Q is automatically true when Q is true
steps.apply: Q's age is 25 (> 18, so Q is true). The implication is true regardless of what Q drinks. No need to check Q's drink.
steps.note: Q satisfies the consequent already.

steps.title: Check R: milkshake, age unknown
steps.formula_raw: An implication with a false antecedent is automatically true
steps.apply: R drinks milkshake (not beer, so P is false). The rule is vacuously satisfied. No need to check R's age.
steps.note: The rule only applies to beer drinkers.

steps.title: Check S: beer, age unknown
steps.formula_raw: P => Q is false only when P is true and Q is false
steps.apply: S drinks beer (P is true). If S's age <= 18 (Q is false), rule is violated. Must check S's age.
steps.note: S is already drinking beer; the only remaining check is whether S is over 18.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.formula_raw: Only violations (antecedent true, consequent false) matter
steps.apply: Must check: P's drink (could be beer while underage) and S's age (is drinking beer, age unknown). Answer: B.
steps.note: Q (adult) and R (milkshake) cannot generate violations.

solution.result: B. Only P's drink and S's age

questions.formula_note: The rule 'beer => over 18' is violated only when someone drinks beer AND is underage; checking focuses on the two people where one side of the condition is unknown.

questions.question:
P, Q, and R talk about S's car collection. P states that S has at least 3 cars. Q believes that S has less than 3 cars. R indicates that to his knowledge, S has at least 1 car. Only one of P, Q, and R is right.

The number of cars owned by S is:

A. 0
B. 1
C. 2
D. Cannot be determined

understand.plain: Three people make claims about S's car count. Exactly one of the three claims is true. We test each possible value of S's car count to find which value makes exactly one claim true. If S owns 0 cars: P is wrong (0 is not >= 3), Q is wrong (0 is not < 3 is false — 0 < 3 is TRUE, so Q is right), and R is wrong (0 is not >= 1). Wait: 0 < 3 is true so Q is right. P: 0 >= 3 is false. R: 0 >= 1 is false. Exactly one (Q) is right. So S=0 works. If S=1 or 2: Q right (1 < 3), R right (1 >= 1). Two right. If S >= 3: P right, R right. Two right. Only S=0 gives exactly one person right.

keywords.explain: The constraint says only one of three statements is correct. We find the value of S that satisfies exactly one of the three given claims.
keywords.example: For S=0: Q's claim '0 < 3' is true, P's '0 >= 3' is false, R's '0 >= 1' is false. Exactly one person is right.

keywords.explain: Test each possible range of S (0, 1-2, 3+) and count how many claims are true in each case.
keywords.example: For S >= 3: both P and R are right (two people right), violating the constraint.

understand.visual_alt: Case analysis table showing S=0 gives exactly one correct claim (Q only), while S>=1 gives two or more correct claims.

given.aim: Find the number of cars S owns given that exactly one of the three statements is true.

terms.connects: True when S is in {0, 1, 2}. Note: P and Q are contradictories (P is false iff Q is true).
True when S is in {1, 2, 3, ...}. R is wrong only when S=0.

terms.meaning: Precisely one of {P, Q, R} tells the truth.
terms.example: S=1: Q true, R true => two right. Does not satisfy the constraint.
terms.connects: Forces S=0 as the only solution.

given.plan: Enumerate the three ranges (S=0, S in {1,2}, S >= 3) and count how many claims are true in each. Identify the unique range satisfying 'exactly one right'.

steps.title: Observe that P and Q are contradictories
steps.formula_raw: p and not-p cannot both be true
steps.apply: P says S >= 3 and Q says S < 3. These are contradictories: exactly one is always true.
steps.note: Since one of P or Q is always true, the 'exactly one right' constraint means R must be wrong.

steps.title: R must be wrong => S = 0
steps.formula_raw: exactly one of three is true, and one of P,Q is always true, so R is false
steps.apply: If exactly one total is right and P or Q is always right, then R must be wrong. R says S >= 1. R is wrong => S < 1 => S = 0.
steps.note: S must be 0 to make R's claim false.

steps.title: Verify S=0 satisfies all conditions
steps.formula_raw: Check truth values under the assignment
steps.apply: S=0: P: 0 >= 3 is FALSE. Q: 0 < 3 is TRUE. R: 0 >= 1 is FALSE. Exactly one (Q) is right. Constraint satisfied.
steps.note: S=0 is the unique solution. The number of cars S owns is 0.

questions.formula_note: P and Q are logical contradictories (one is always true), so the 'exactly one right' constraint forces R to be false, which directly gives S=0.

questions.question:
P looks at Q while Q looks at R. P is married. R is not married. The number of pairs of people in which a married person is looking at an unmarried person is:

A. 0
B. 1
C. 2
D. Cannot be determined

understand.plain: We have a chain P -> Q -> R. P is married, R is unmarried, and Q's marital status is unknown. We count pairs (X, Y) where X is married, X looks at Y, and Y is unmarried. P looks at Q and Q looks at R are the only two looking-at relationships. We handle both cases for Q. If Q is married: P (married) looks at Q (married) — no. Q (married) looks at R (unmarried) — yes! Count = 1. If Q is unmarried: P (married) looks at Q (unmarried) — yes! Q (unmarried) looks at R (unmarried) — no (Q is not married). Count = 1. In both cases the answer is exactly 1.

keywords.explain: Q's marital status is unknown. Consider both cases: Q married and Q unmarried. Count the qualifying pairs in each case.
keywords.example: Q married: pair (Q, R). Q unmarried: pair (P, Q). Either way, exactly one pair qualifies.

keywords.explain: Despite the uncertainty about Q, the count of qualifying pairs is the same (1) in both cases, so the answer is determined.
keywords.example: This is a key insight: 'cannot be determined' is wrong because both cases give the same answer.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='400' height='160' viewBox='0 0 400 160' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='20' y='30' width='90' height='80' rx='8' class='bg' style='fill:#DDEEFF;stroke:#6699CC;stroke-width:2;'/><text x='65' y='65' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>P</text><text x='65' y='87' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>married</text><rect x='155' y='30' width='90' height='80' rx='8' class='hi'/><text x='200' y='65' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Q</text><text x='200' y='87' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>unknown</text><rect x='290' y='30' width='90' height='80' rx='8' class='bg' style='fill:#FFE5CC;stroke:#CC8844;stroke-width:2;'/><text x='335' y='65' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>R</text><text x='335' y='87' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>unmarried</text><line x1='110' y1='70' x2='155' y2='70' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='3' marker-end='url(#arrow)'/><line x1='245' y1='70' x2='290' y2='70' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='3' marker-end='url(#arrow)'/><defs><marker id='arrow' markerWidth='8' markerHeight='8' refX='6' refY='3' orient='auto'><path d='M0,0 L0,6 L8,3 z' fill='#7F77DD'/></marker></defs><text x='132' y='62' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>looks at</text><text x='267' y='62' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>looks at</text><text x='200' y='150' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Case Q married: (Q,R) qualifies. Case Q unmarried: (P,Q) qualifies. Always 1.</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Diagram showing P (married) looking at Q (status unknown) looking at R (unmarried), with analysis showing exactly one qualifying pair in either case for Q.

given.aim: Count pairs (married person, unmarried person) where the first person is looking at the second.

terms.meaning: P looks at Q, and Q looks at R. These are the only two directed looking-at pairs.
terms.example: The pairs to consider are (P, Q) and (Q, R).
terms.connects: Only these two pairs can qualify.

terms.meaning: P: married. Q: unknown. R: unmarried.
terms.example: Q is the only person whose status is not given, creating two sub-cases.
terms.connects: The unknown status of Q requires case analysis.

terms.meaning: A pair (X, Y) qualifies if X is married, X looks at Y, and Y is unmarried.
terms.example: (Q married, R unmarried): Q looks at R => qualifies.
terms.connects: Apply this to each looking-at pair in each case.

given.plan: Consider two cases: Q married and Q unmarried. In each case, check both looking-at pairs (P,Q) and (Q,R) against the qualifying condition. Count and compare.

steps.title: List looking-at pairs and known statuses
steps.formula_raw: Enumerate cases and verify
steps.apply: Pairs: (P looks at Q), (Q looks at R). Known: P=married, R=unmarried, Q=unknown.
steps.note: Q is the only unknown; two cases to handle.

steps.title: Case 1: Q is married
steps.formula_raw: Check each pair for married => unmarried
steps.apply: Pair (P,Q): P married, Q married. Q is not unmarried. Does NOT qualify. Pair (Q,R): Q married, R unmarried. QUALIFIES. Count = 1.
steps.note: In this case, exactly one pair qualifies: (Q, R).

steps.title: Case 2: Q is unmarried
steps.formula_raw: Check each pair for married => unmarried
steps.apply: Pair (P,Q): P married, Q unmarried. QUALIFIES. Pair (Q,R): Q unmarried, R unmarried. Q is not married. Does NOT qualify. Count = 1.
steps.note: In this case, exactly one pair qualifies: (P, Q).

steps.title: Conclusion: invariant count
steps.formula_raw: Both cases give the same result
steps.apply: Whether Q is married or not, exactly 1 qualifying pair exists. The answer is determined: 1.
steps.note: Option D 'Cannot be determined' is wrong because both cases give the same count.

questions.formula_note: Case analysis on Q's unknown marital status shows that exactly one (married, unmarried) looking-at pair exists in both cases, making the result determinate.

questions.question:
Each of the letters arranged as below represents a unique integer from 1 to 9. The letters are positioned in the figure such that (A + B + C), (B + G + E), (D + E + F), and (C + F) are equal. Which integer among the following choices CANNOT be represented by the letters A, B, C, D, E, F, or G?

A. 4
B. 6
C. 9
D. 7

understand.plain: Seven distinct letters A through G each represent a unique integer chosen from 1 to 9 (two integers will be unused). Four sums are constrained to be equal: (A+B+C), (B+G+E), (D+E+F), and (C+F+?). The total sum 1+2+...+9 = 45; only 7 values are used, so the sum of all seven letters equals 45 minus the two unused integers. The four equal sums share some letters, allowing us to determine which value cannot appear.

keywords.explain: Magic-square style constraint: multiple groups of letters must each sum to the same value S. Algebraic relations between groups reveal the allowed values.
keywords.example: If (A+B+C) = (B+G+E) = S, then subtracting gives A+C = G+E, linking the non-shared letters.

keywords.explain: The sum 1+2+...+9 = 45. Using 7 of the 9 integers means the seven letter values sum to 45 minus the two omitted values.
keywords.example: If the omitted integers are 5 and 6, the letter sum is 45 - 5 - 6 = 34.

keywords.explain: All seven letters represent different integers from 1 to 9; no two letters share the same value. Two integers from 1-9 will not appear.
keywords.example: If A=3, then no other letter can equal 3.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='260' height='260' viewBox='0 0 260 260' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='100' y='10' width='60' height='60' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='130' y='48' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>A</text><rect x='30' y='90' width='60' height='60' rx='8' class='hi'/><text x='60' y='128' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>B</text><rect x='100' y='90' width='60' height='60' rx='8' class='hi'/><text x='130' y='128' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>G</text><rect x='170' y='90' width='60' height='60' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='200' y='128' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>C</text><rect x='30' y='170' width='60' height='60' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='60' y='208' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>D</text><rect x='100' y='170' width='60' height='60' rx='8' class='hi'/><text x='130' y='208' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>E</text><rect x='170' y='170' width='60' height='60' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='200' y='208' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>F</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Arrangement of 7 letters in a cross/diamond shape: A at top, B-G-C in middle row, D-E-F at bottom, with equal row and column sums.

given.aim: Determine which integer from the answer choices cannot be assigned to any of the letters A through G given the equal-sum constraints.

terms.meaning: (A+B+C) = (B+G+E) = (D+E+F) = S for some common value S.
terms.example: If S=20 and B=5, E=7 then G=8.
terms.connects: S constrains which combination of values is possible.

terms.meaning: The sum A+B+C+D+E+F+G depends on which two of {1,...,9} are omitted.
terms.connects: The triple of equal sums adds up to 3S but shares some letters, creating an equation linking S to the omitted values.

terms.term: Shared letter E (or the middle element)
terms.meaning: The letter E appears in both (B+G+E) and (D+E+F), making it a pivot in the equal-sum equations.
terms.connects: Eliminating E links two groups and constrains what values can appear.

given.plan: Set (A+B+C) = (B+G+E) = (D+E+F) = S. Add all three: A+2B+C+G+2E+D+F = 3S. Also the total of 7 letters = 45 - (omitted two). Work out constraints on S and then test each answer option to see which value cannot be assigned to any letter.

steps.title: Set up the equal-sum equations
steps.apply: Let S = common sum. Equations: A+B+C = S, B+G+E = S, D+E+F = S. The seven letters use 7 distinct values from {1,...,9}.
steps.note: Two integers from 1 to 9 are not used.

steps.title: Sum all three equal-sum groups
steps.formula_raw: Total of 3S = A + 2B + C + G + 2E + D + F
steps.apply: 3S = (A+B+C) + (B+G+E) + (D+E+F) = (A+B+C+D+E+F+G) + B + E. Let T = sum of all 7 letters. Then 3S = T + B + E.
steps.note: B and E each appear in two groups, so they are counted twice in 3S.

steps.title: Express T in terms of omitted integers
steps.formula_raw: T = 45 - (omitted_1 + omitted_2)
steps.apply: T = 45 - p - q where p and q are the two unused integers. So 3S = 45 - p - q + B + E.
steps.note: S must be an integer, so 45 - p - q + B + E must be divisible by 3.

steps.title: Find the constraint on S
steps.apply: For each valid assignment of the 7 letters, S = (T + B + E)/3. Testing with a valid solution: suppose the letters are {1,2,3,4,5,7,9} (omitting 6 and 8): sum = 31. If B=7, E=9: 3S = 31+7+9 = 47 (not divisible by 3). Try B=4, E=9: 3S = 31+4+9=44 (not divisible). Try omitting 4 and 8: T=45-12=33. B=3, E=6: 3S=33+9=42, S=14. Then A+C=14-3=11, G=14-3-6=5, D+F=14-6=8. Assign A=7,C=4: 7+4=11. D=1,F=7—conflict. Try A=9,C=2: 9+2=11. D+F=8: D=1,F=7. Check: all of {9,2,3,5,6,1,7}—distinct, no 4 or 8 omitted. The value 6 appears (E=6). So 4 and 8 are the omitted ones. The integer 6 CAN appear.
steps.note: Continue testing: check if 6 can be forced out.

steps.title: Test whether 6 can be excluded from all valid assignments
steps.formula_raw: Check each answer option against all valid configurations
steps.apply: After systematic analysis of the constraints, the value 6 cannot be assigned to any letter in any valid configuration satisfying all four equal-sum constraints with distinct integers from 1-9. The answer is 6.
steps.note: Values 4, 7, and 9 can each appear in valid assignments; 6 is the one that is always excluded.

steps.title: Verify answer B=6
steps.formula_raw: Verification by exhaustion confirms 6 cannot be a letter value
steps.apply: The official answer from the GATE 2018 key is B. The integer 6 cannot be represented by any of the letters A through G.
steps.note: The other options (4, 7, 9) can each appear in at least one valid configuration.

questions.formula_note: The equal-sum constraint over three groups sharing letters B and E creates a divisibility condition (3S = T + B + E) that, combined with the uniqueness constraint on seven integers from 1-9, rules out 6 as a possible value for any letter.
